food_name: Indian Mustard Cabbage Leaf (Kai Choy), Raw
aliases: Indian Mustard Cabbage Leaf (Kai Choy), Raw

english: Indian Mustard Cabbage Leaf (Kai Choy), Raw

region_of_origin: South Asia / Southeast Asia (widely cultivated across Singapore, Malaysia, China, India)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw kai choy is approximately 56 g (1 cup chopped), commonly used as a stir-fry vegetable or blanched side dish in Singaporean and Southeast Asian meals.

nutrient_source.title: Indian Mustard Cabbage Leaf (Kai Choy), Raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Indian mustard cabbage leaf (Kai choy), raw (cr_id F14010024). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11270 (mustard greens, raw) which reports 0.03 g saturated fat per 100 g, rounded to 0.0 g. Vitamin C estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 11270 at 70 mg/100 g, consistent with the input record's health_suitability note.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Leafy Vegetables — Analogous Estimate Based on Mustard Greens / Leafy Brassica Category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kai choy (Brassica juncea, raw) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with other raw non-starchy leafy Brassica vegetables (e.g., spinach, cabbage, bok choy) which consistently test at GI < 20 due to negligible available carbohydrate (0.7 g/100 g). Glycemic load per 56 g serving = (15 × 0.7 × 0.56) / 100 = 0.06, rounded to 0.1. This is consistent with standard clinical guidance that non-starchy leafy vegetables have negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely low sodium (3 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (450 mg/100 g) yielding an outstanding Na:K ratio supportive of DASH diet targets
hypertension.guidance: Raw kai choy is highly suitable for hypertension management; its sodium-to-potassium ratio strongly supports blood pressure reduction per DASH diet principles, but patients should avoid adding salt, soy sauce, or high-sodium condiments during cooking, and must avoid pickled/preserved kai choy (ham choy) which contains very high sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low available carbohydrate (0.7 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~15), negligible glycemic load per serving, and meaningful dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) supporting postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw kai choy is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its negligible glycemic load and high fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio; patients should avoid high-sugar cooking sauces (oyster sauce, sweet soy) and check labels on pickled versions for added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat, high potassium (450 mg/100 g), and sulforaphane-yielding cruciferous phytochemicals (Brassica juncea) with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw kai choy is cardioprotective and suitable for cardiovascular disease patients; its zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat, high potassium, and cruciferous anti-inflammatory compounds support vascular health, and it should be prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces to preserve its cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cruciferous sulforaphane compounds, high vitamin C (~70 mg/100 g), and high calcium (130 mg/100 g) supporting bone protection in RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kai choy is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory sulforaphane content and high calcium supporting bone density; avoid pickled/preserved versions (ham choy) which worsen RA-related tissue swelling, and lightly cook to preserve vitamin C and sulforaphane.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Indian mustard cabbage leaf, commonly known as kai choy (芥菜) in Singapore and Southeast Asia, is the raw leaf of Brassica juncea, a cruciferous leafy vegetable widely cultivated across South and Southeast Asia. The leaves are bright green with tender but firm stems, offering a crisp texture and a mild, slightly peppery flavour characteristic of the mustard family. In Singapore, kai choy is a staple vegetable used in stir-fries, soups, braised dishes, and as a blanched side vegetable. It is also the base ingredient for ham choy (pickled mustard greens), a preserved form with very different nutritional properties. Nutritionally, raw kai choy is exceptionally low in calories (18 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrates (0.7 g/100 g), making it one of the most diabetes-friendly vegetables available. It provides a notable 2.3 g protein per 100 g for a leafy vegetable, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and an outstanding potassium content of 450 mg/100 g against only 3 mg sodium, making it ideal for hypertension management under DASH diet principles. Its calcium content (130 mg/100 g) is high for a leafy vegetable and supports bone health. As a Brassica, it contains sulforaphane and glucosinolates with anti-inflammatory and potential cardioprotective properties. Vitamin C content is approximately 70 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low) due to negligible available carbohydrate, with a glycemic load per serving approaching zero. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis when prepared without high-sodium condiments or sauces.

How much calcium is in kai choy?
What is the difference between kai choy and ham choy?
